Although, strictly speaking, redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons, the most common form of redox reaction involves the exchange of hydrogen atoms between molecules (where the molecule has been oxidized, the reaction can therefore be further classified as a *dehydrogenation* reaction). In such cases, the reaction is still an example of a redox reaction because, every time a hydrogen atom leaves a molecule, it does so with an electron attached. For this reason, hydrogen atoms are often referred to as *reducing equivalents*, because they are equivalent to electrons (recall from Figure 3.1 that hydrogen atoms have a single proton and electron).

Redox reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms are essentially the underpinning basis for exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise, because it is the transfer of hydrogen atoms from the food we eat to oxygen molecules (to produce water) which produces the energy store (ATP) to fuel our muscles during exercise. In such circumstances, oxygen is said to be the final *electron acceptor*, as it undergoes a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water. Collectively, this process is known as *oxidative phosphorylation* and we will cover this in detail in Chapter 5.

## Definition

Redox reactions are reactions that strictly involve the transfer of electrons between molecules. The most common biological form involves the exchange of hydrogen atoms between molecules; when a molecule loses hydrogen atoms it is said to be oxidized, and such a reaction can be further classified as a *dehydrogenation* reaction. The reaction still qualifies as redox because each hydrogen atom departs with its electron attached.

## Core Principles

- Every hydrogen atom transferred in a redox reaction carries its electron with it (a hydrogen atom = one proton + one electron), so hydrogen transfer is electrochemically equivalent to electron transfer.
- For this reason, hydrogen atoms are often called *reducing equivalents*.
- Loss of hydrogen atoms from a molecule constitutes oxidation (dehydrogenation); gain of hydrogen atoms constitutes reduction.
- In aerobic metabolism, oxygen serves as the final *electron acceptor*, undergoing a four-electron reduction (accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water.
- The transfer of hydrogen atoms from food to oxygen is the energetic basis for ATP production that fuels muscle during endurance exercise.

## Sub-concepts

- *Dehydrogenation*: a sub-class of redox reaction in which the oxidized molecule loses hydrogen atoms.
- *Reducing equivalents*: hydrogen atoms acting as carriers of electrons in biological redox chemistry.
- *Electron acceptor*: a molecule that accepts electrons (or hydrogen atoms) in a redox reaction; oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor in aerobic metabolism.
- *Oxidative phosphorylation*: the collective process by which hydrogen atoms from food are transferred to oxygen, producing water and generating ATP.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Redox reactions involving hydrogen transfer underpin exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise: hydrogen atoms from ingested food are ultimately transferred to oxygen to form water, producing the ATP that fuels skeletal muscle contraction.
- Understanding redox chemistry is foundational for interpreting oxidative phosphorylation and aerobic ATP supply during prolonged exercise.
